sentences.text: [LAUGHS]
Sam Wise, S-A-M W-I-S-E.
The name of the birth?
In-- in Yiddish, [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE].
December the 15th, 1915.
In a little island, in a little-- little shtetl.
The shtel called Vendziogola.
Who made up my family?
My father, mother, grandfather, grandmother.
My grandfather or my father?
My grandfather was Ruven.
And my grandmother was Chaya.
And then my father's was Abram.
And my mother was Basia.
What else?
Yeah.
My older sister was Asna.
My second one, my brother was Issak.
Later, my sister was Sarah.
And my brother again was Mottel.
And my sister again was Pesa.
And my little brother was the last one, Berele.
The shtetl was like a village.
You knew everybody like in was-- was in a country.
There was only about 200 families, Jewish people.
And everybody was together, all around.
Between-- between the Gentile people, you know what-- what it was, a country.
You know.
The Jewish community was a shul.
There was a lot of organizations, you know, Jewish organizations.
There was-- there was Hashomer Hatzair.
There was Betar.
There was-- there was Hasidic.
And there was a shul, a big shul, only one, with a rabbi, with a Hazim, like all the Yiddish shtetl in Europe.
Everybody had a piece of land, you know.
And they raised, by himself, 90% they raise by himself, food.
Like potatoes, like corn.
Like-- like-- and we got the cows.
Everybody got their own cow, or two cows, or three.
And sheep and-- and the ducks, and the geese, and their own eggs, even.
They brought chicken.
Everything was, you know, natural.
My daddy, we had a little general store.
From everything, a little, some from food, some anything else, you know.
Yiddish, Hebrew.
And the Lithuanian.
Religious Orthodox.
Strictly Orthodox.
The what?
Oh, yeah.
My father davened three times a day.
And my mother would light the candles every Friday night.
And it was really religious.
And come Saturday, you didn't do-- nobody did nothing in the world.
When it comes Saturday, Saturday night, they make the Havdalah.
They come and-- they come back in shul-- from shul, they sang [? the zemiras, ?] you know, all together, like when they make the Havdalah.
You know.
And this is the way that he'd-- he'd daven three times a day.
I went in a public school.
And I went in a cheder.
OK.
The cheder first when I'd been six years.
I still remember how the father took me in the tallit.
Only he took me on the hand.
And he brought me in his hand in cheder, the first time in this cheder.
And he put his hand on my head, and he [NON-ENGLISH] me.
You know, he make a prayer on my head.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]
Oh, the-- the cheder was about 30, 35.
In the cheder-- I'd been in-- I'd been in public school and the cheder, too.
I'd been in public school for to learn Hebrew and Yiddish and Lithuanian.
This is the main Lithuanian language.
And later, in the night time, we went to cheder again to learn more about the Judaism, you know, like Humash, like [INAUDIBLE], like Tanakh, everything.
Yeah?
Like, you know, like in-- every-- every shtetl had their little-- not too much anti-Semitism.
But, you know, all the Jewish people live together.
Everybody make a-- a living with the Gentile people from the country.
You know.
We bought from them some-- some chickens, some cows, some everything.
And-- and they bought from the-- the-- a lot of Jewish people bought from these stores what they need for the-- for the country, for the farms.
No.
The what?
Uh-huh.
Uh-huh.
Yeah, my mother.
My mother used to sing.
My mother used to dance with me, everything.
I've been-- I've been [INAUDIBLE] our family.
[LAUGHS]
I'd been a traveler salesman.
I'd been in the army in Lithuania.
Nothing.
When you came 20-- 21 years, you got to go in the army.
I stayed there two years.
And that's all.
They learned you how, you know, how to be a soldier.
How to kill, how to shoot, how to be in the war, how to act in the war.
Everything, they learn you.
Yeah.
I-- later they learned me how to make signals, you know, with a-- with a-- with a bugle, you know, like to attack or to lay down or to run or something.
I got-- I got to make signals.
They learned me how to do signals.
There was plenty of Jews in the army.
Yeah.
I had-- I tell you what, I had one time in the army, is right-- I had one not Jewish fellow, and he called me one time not by my name.
He called me dirty Jew, dirty Jew, dirty Jew.
And one time I got mad, and-- and I had, you know, my-- we used to, the-- the army, we used to go, when they're going out even for home, sometimes for holiday or something.
You got to-- you got to wear with you the stick.
You know, the-- what they put on the-- on the-- on the rifle.
You know, what are they calling this?
The-- I don't know how they're calling this in English.
You know, what they're sticking in.
And I took it one time I got mad.
When I got in a fight.
And I knocked him here.
And they put me for three weeks in jail.
And they put him in hospital for-- for-- for two months, maybe.
And I never heard from him, not one word.
Everybody scared to tell me I'm a dirty Jew or something like this.
And that's what-- I was happy what I did this.
I didn't care what I'd been-- in the army I'd been three weeks in jail.
But I did one time what I got to do.
A normal life.
I'd been a salesman.
No, we heard about Nazism.
You know we heard first about what was in  Poland When the Poland Jewish, some of them would run away, you know, from the Nazis.
They came to Lithuania, you know, to hide here himself.
And every Jewish helped out a little bit.
Come on, when they're coming, we want to save your life from Hitler.
And that's all.
We didn't-- never-- didn't believe this Hitler would come sometime to Lithuania.
And that's what we helped out the-- the Poland Jewish when the Nazis occupied Poland.
But we never believed he'd come in-- in Lithuania because Lithuania was already-- was Russian.
Right-- about Nazism?
We found out before, a long time before.
But when they came, when they  occupied Poland, this was the worst.
What we came-- what  we know already was going on there-- about the ghetto, about the-- about the yellow stars, with everything.
It was in '30-- '34, I think. '35 or something like this.
I don't remember the years.
Yeah.
Hashomer Hatzair, that's a-- a Zionist organization.
Me and my wife was belong to the same organization.
I'd been 16, 17 years old.
Zionism.
You know, Zionism.
We-- we-- we dreamed to go to Israel, you know, or to have our own country like-- like all-- like all the nations in the world.
And that's what we-- that's what-- that's what we did.
After the army, back-- I go back.
I had my job again, what I had before.
And I-- I have a normal life.
In Lithuania.
In Kovno.
That's a big town.
That's the-- that's the capital for Lithuania.
From the shtetl in the army.
And I'd been on the leave later, we're moving to Kovno.
You know.
And in Kovno, we live together and made a family.
No, there didn't was a ghetto yet, you know, because the Russians occupied Lithuania.
And the Russians didn't make any ghettos.
Hm?
We moved to Kovno because my-- my brother and my sister and me, we left the shtetl.
You know.
And we want a better life, a more educational life, a more activity life with more people.
And we moved.
We-- we worked together, all of them, in the big town.
And later, we bring our parents there, and we lived together.
That was in 1932, '33, something like this.
Yeah.
Sure.
Yeah, I been in the ghetto.
Everybody got to go-- got to go in the ghetto.
They-- they make us to go in the ghetto, to leave everything to go in the ghetto.
And in the ghetto they put me, the whole ghetto was around with-- with barbed wires.
You know.
We couldn't go in or go.
And nobody can go in or go out without-- without-- And this was-- this was the ghetto.
You got to  leave everything and to stay there in ghetto.
And we been in the ghetto, like three, four families in one room.
They didn't care if you got a leave-- if you got a room or you don't got a room.
They keep us like-- like animals.
The Germans.
Nobody else except the Germans.
They make the ghettos.
And they made the concentration camps.
In Kovno, there was a-- like here, is Atlanta, let's say.
The Decatur or Marietta, there it is Slobodka.
It was Slobodka.
There what we live is Slobodka.
What do you mean who takes?
It was announced.
They make announcement.
All the Jewish people got to go this and then date in ghetto.
Got to go in the ghetto, and who don't do it, who be late, he's gonna be shot.
They're gonna be killed.
We grab-- we took only what we can handle in hand.
That's all.
We got to leave everything.
Your clothes, what you can take.
Anything that you can.
Clothes, the main thing is clothes or shoes.
I couldn't take a bed.
Or I couldn't take-- we couldn't take any furniture.
We took anything what you need is right away.
The first thing is clothes, shoes, clothing.
No.
The ghetto was-- the ghetto-- they make the ghetto in Slobodka.
There where we used to live.
And this, what we was-- lucky.
What we have the furniture, too, with everything.
You know, but we didn't move nothing.
But who lived in Kovno, when they got to go in the ghetto, they couldn't carry everything.
Only-- only that what you can take in your hand.
I'd-- I'd been in Slobodka.
The ghetto was in Slobodka.
The took the whole like [NON-ENGLISH], like Decatur, you understand?
This is Slobodka.
And there is where they make all Slobodka.
They make a ghetto from them.
Slobodka ghetto.
Kovno didn't was a ghetto.
This belonged to Kovno, you know, Slobodka.
In the ghetto before, right now when the-- when the-- the Nazis came in.
It's before the [INAUDIBLE] over there.
It's the anti-Semitic, you know, the-- the Lithuanian.
They kill more Jewish than the Germans.
They came, and they robbed, and they killed, and they did it worse than the Germans.
Yeah.
All of them.
No, I didn't leave by myself, but they took us every day in the morning, about 3 o'clock, 4 o'clock in the morning.
They took us to any kind of work.
The Germans came, and they took 300 people, 500 people to work on a-- on an airport or to dig ditch, to dig, to do all kind of hard work.
Do-- do you got to come in a place.
All of them got to go in the morning to say, everybody come on them-- their place.
And the Germans select how many people they need to work-- 100 people here, 100 people there, 100 people in the other place.
And they took us.
They took like-- like-- I didn't select.
I was placed where to go.
Where they take you, you got to go.
And you couldn't say no.
They who say no to-- you were right there.
What they give you.
They give you some 100 gram for a person a day.
Bread on-- on a-- on a soup, you know.
Some kind of soup from the horse meat.
You know.
Later, they give you like a piece of-- like a piece of margarine one day.
You got a piece of marmalade, something like this.
But this is-- this-- people died every day like-- like flies, you know, from hunger.
Especially children.
Especially elderly people where they couldn't work.
And later, they took a hospital where older people was and they-- and they burn up the doctors.
In the ghetto, they burn up the doctors with the nurses with all the-- all-- all the elderly people.
And they bun them up them alive.
They told the doctors not to leave, not to leave the hospital, and the nurses not to leave the hospital.
If somebody-- they're going to leave, he'd-- he'd be shot.
And they put the gasoline.
And they burned up the whole hospital.
Everybody see this.
Everybody see how they burn up this.
This was in ghetto.
What you can do?
You are like-- you are like animals between barbed wires.
What you can do?
On this I was lucky.
Somebody, a lot  of us was lucky would go into the  work.
You know.
When they go into work, you took something.
But you had sometimes, you had a little vase, or you had an extra shirt.
You know.
And with the Gentile people, you trade them for a loaf of bread, for a piece of butter, or a piece of meat, some kind.
And you got to hide this.
Because in the ghetto, when you come back to work, and they found out you've got something, you get killed.
If you got-- I remember when I-- I brought sometimes for a shirt, I had a piece of meat, you know, raw meat.
And I dropped down the meat around my legs.
And they searched me, they didn't search-- the-- I took a chance.
Because everybody knows that tomorrow, or next day, you get killed.
Everybody knew it.
Nobody would be alive.
But we took a chance.
And this what keep up my family when my father couldn't go to the work.
He was already old.
And it happened someday I pushed by myself into-- go into work.
And I bringing something to-- to trade for a piece of bread, for a piece of butter, a piece-- something flour, a little bit.
And I wrap up everywhere I can.
And I then go through-- to the-- to the-- to the gate, to the ghetto.
And this what I keep up my parents, you know.
With everything little by little, we got-- we got to manage.
Germans.
The Germans?
How I know?
I never-- I didn't want to know them.
The Germans-- I never see Germans in my life in Lithuania.
The German army.
The German army, the SS.
The SS, they stay by the ghetto.
They stay by the tower.
And they let you out, and they let you in.
And they search you every day.
But sometimes, it was, in the ghetto, Yiddish police, you know.
But the Jew-- the Yiddish police take care inside in the ghetto.
But they have to wear like-- like all of we got to wear, the star.
The yellow star got to wear-- even a-- a baby from three months, even, you got to wear a star.
Yeah, sure I know them.
And some of the Jewish police, we make with them-- we make with them-- he give something to the German police, you know, like a-- like a ring, something somebody had.
Or somebody had a little vase or something like this.
On the morning, let him back through.
Let him through.
Let him through.
Let him through.
You see?
Who?
Yeah.
There was uniforms with a Yiddish-- with a Jewish star, you know.
With a helmet with a Jewish star.
A uniform they had.
What the-- the Germans make him to do it.
Yeah, sure.
Sure, but they didn't have any ammunition.
They didn't have any ammunition, only with a-- a-- a stick.
You know.
A little cane to beat somebody or something like this, but no-- no ammunition.
